summary: Gruber dissects Apple's September 2007 iPod lineup refresh, focusing on the strategic gaps between the iPod Touch and iPhone. He argues that Apple deliberately hobbled the iPod Touch by excluding Mail, Maps, and other iPhone apps to protect iPhone's premium positioning. He notes the iPod Shuffle's stasis suggests no competitive pressure at the low end, finds the new Nano's proportions awkward despite its thinness, and observes the iPod Classic's 160GB marks a new era of carry-your-whole-library convenience. He's particularly sharp on Jobs's keynote sleight of hand in pitching Safari's inclusion to distract from Mail's exclusion, and skeptical of the Starbucks Wi-Fi deal's actual value.
key_points: Apple strategically withheld iPhone apps from the iPod Touch to maintain the iPhone as the superior device, following the principle that it's easier to add features later than remove them
Jobs used keynote misdirection to present Safari's inclusion on the iPod Touch as a bonus feature, drawing attention away from the conspicuous absence of Mail
The iPod Touch lacks the iPhone's headphone clicker, making it impractical as a pocket music player — a fundamental iPod use case
Apple's accounting treatment of iPod Touch sales (not subscription-based like iPhone) signals they may not plan free feature updates
The iTunes Wi-Fi Music Store name is a deliberate statement of independence from mobile carriers, emphasizing Apple controls the storefront
money_quotes: It's easier to lower prices than to raise them; it's easier to add features than to take existing features away.
What a browser needs is a network, but Jobs pitched it as the network needing the browser.

It would have proved disastrous if Apple had shipped the iPod Touch with more of the iPhone's features, then decided it was too good compared to the iPhone, and then tried to take some of them away in a future software update.
160 GB is more storage than many personal computers offer. Fill it up with typical MP3 or AAC audio, and it would take three months to listen to it all.
It's a big 'fuck you' to the mobile network carriers, a way of emphasizing that this store is Apple's, not theirs.
Ooh, free Wi-Fi that I can only use to buy music from you. Thanks!
Not to mention the seemingly spiteful absence of event creation in the Calendar app on the iPod Touch.
There's been a lot less bitching about the Touch not including Mail than I expected — I think that's at least partly attributable to the way Jobs presented the inclusion of Safari.
key_insight: Apple deliberately crippled the iPod Touch relative to the iPhone not out of technical limitation but as strategic product positioning, betting it's safer to withhold features you can add later than to ship too much and face the backlash of taking them away.
